title: Multi-Agent Orchestration
summary: Coordinate several specialized agents to solve one problem — an orchestrator delegates to workers, agents run in parallel, or they debate and critique each other to reach a better answer.
analogy: A film production. A director (orchestrator) doesn't shoot, light, and score the movie alone — they coordinate specialists: cinematographer, editor, composer. Each is excellent at one craft; the director composes them into a coherent whole.
problem: One agent with one giant prompt and a huge tool belt becomes a confused generalist: its context overflows, tool selection degrades, and a single reasoning thread can't pursue several sub-problems at once.
forces: Specialization beats generalization, but specialists must be coordinated.
Some subtasks are independent and could run in parallel for speed.
Every agent boundary adds communication overhead and failure points.
More agents multiply cost and make the system harder to debug.
A single critic can catch errors a lone author misses.
solution: Decompose the problem across multiple agents with distinct roles, coordinated by a topology: an orchestrator/supervisor that delegates to workers and integrates results, parallel agents that fan out independent subtasks, or debate/critique setups where agents review each other's work.
tradeoffs: Much higher token cost and latency than a single agent — Anthropic notes multi-agent can burn ~15x the tokens.
Coordination and message-passing add complexity and new failure modes.
Harder to debug: errors hide in inter-agent handoffs.
Often a well-built single agent is enough — don't reach for a swarm by default.
body:
## First principles
As a task grows, one agent hits ceilings: its **context window** fills with too many instructions and tool results, **tool selection** gets noisy among dozens of options, and a single reasoning thread can't explore multiple sub-problems simultaneously. These are the same limits a single overloaded person hits — and humans solve them the same way: **divide labor and coordinate**.

Multi-agent orchestration applies division of labor to LLMs. Each agent gets a **focused role, a smaller prompt, and a tighter toolset**, so it performs better; a coordination structure stitches their work together.

## Common topologies (mental model)
- **Orchestrator–worker (supervisor)**: a lead agent breaks the task into subtasks and delegates each to a worker, then synthesizes results. The dominant production pattern (LangGraph supervisor, Anthropic's research system).
- **Parallelization**: fan out independent subtasks to agents at once, then aggregate (voting, merging). Faster for separable work.
- **Debate / critique**: multiple agents argue or one critiques another to surface errors and improve answers.

The diagram shows the supervisor topology fanning out to workers and gathering results.

## Real examples
- **Anthropic's multi-agent research system**: a lead agent spawns subagents that search in parallel and report back, improving over single-agent search — but using far more tokens.
- **LangGraph supervisor / swarm**: framework primitives for a supervisor that routes to specialized worker agents.
- **AutoGen / CrewAI**: frameworks for role-based agent teams that converse to solve tasks.

## When to use it
- The task has **separable subtasks** or benefits from **parallel exploration**.
- You need **specialist** behavior that one prompt can't cover well.
- Adversarial **critique** would catch errors (pair with reflection).

## Watch out for
- **Cost/latency explosion**: only go multi-agent when the value clearly beats a single agent.
- **Coordination bugs**: define crisp interfaces between agents.
- **Premature complexity**: start single-agent; split only when you hit real limits.
diagram:
flowchart TD
  G([Task]) --> S[Supervisor /\norchestrator]
  S -->|delegate| W1[Worker A\n(research)]
  S -->|delegate| W2[Worker B\n(code)]
  S -->|delegate| W3[Worker C\n(review)]
  W1 --> S2[Integrate /\nsynthesize]
  W2 --> S2
  W3 --> S2
  S2 --> F([Final result])

examples.what: A lead agent orchestrates parallel subagents that each search and report back; it beat single-agent on breadth but used roughly 15x the tokens.
Provides supervisor and swarm multi-agent primitives so a coordinator can route work to specialized worker agents.

drills.q: Why split a hard task across multiple specialized agents instead of one big agent?
drills.a: Each agent gets a focused role, smaller prompt, and tighter toolset, avoiding context overflow and poor tool selection, and independent subtasks can run in parallel.

drills.q: Name the three common multi-agent topologies.
drills.a: Orchestrator-worker (supervisor), parallelization (fan out then aggregate), and debate/critique.

drills.q: What is the biggest cost of going multi-agent?
drills.a: Greatly increased token cost and latency (Anthropic reported ~15x) plus coordination complexity and harder debugging — so use it only when the value clearly justifies it.

teachItBack: Multi-agent orchestration solves big tasks by dividing labor among specialized agents and coordinating them, usually with a supervisor that delegates subtasks to workers and integrates their results, or by running agents in parallel or having them debate and critique. Each agent gets a focused role and toolset, which beats one overloaded generalist agent. The price is much higher token cost and latency plus coordination complexity, so you only reach for it when a single well-built agent genuinely can't cope.

sources.title: Anthropic — How we built our multi-agent research system

glossary.definition: A topology where a lead agent delegates subtasks to worker agents and synthesizes their outputs.
A coordinating agent that routes work to specialized sub-agents and integrates the results.
Running independent subtasks across multiple agents simultaneously, then aggregating the outputs.
A multi-agent setup where agents argue or review each other's work to surface errors and improve quality.

quiz.q: What is the most common production multi-agent topology?
quiz.explanation: A supervisor/orchestrator delegating subtasks to specialized workers and integrating results is the dominant pattern.

quiz.q: What is the main reason NOT to default to multi-agent systems?
choices: They greatly increase token cost, latency, and coordination complexity
They only work with one model
quiz.explanation: Multi-agent systems can use many times more tokens and add coordination overhead, so a single agent is often the better default.
